name: NASA-Prompt-ScientificTechnicalPlan
description: NASA Scientific/Technical/Management Plan - goals, significance, NASA relevance, technical approach, methodology

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the SCIENTIFIC/TECHNICAL/MANAGEMENT PLAN section of a NASA grant proposal submitted in response to a NASA Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO). INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all scientific content, prior results, technical capabilities, and methodology. - {{grant_info}}: The specific NASA NOFO description, including Mission Directorate (Science, Human Exploration, Aeronautics Research, or Space Technology), program objectives, and evaluation criteria. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Scientific/Technical/Management Plan. Do NOT generate the Proposal Summary/Abstract, Data Management Plan, Budget, Biographical Sketches, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. NOTE: These prompts are a best-effort starting point based on the NASA Guidebook for Proposers (May 2021 edition). The researcher should review and update all content to reflect their specific work, institutional context, and the latest NOFO requirements. SECTION STRUCTURE (per NASA Guidebook Section 2.13): 1. GOALS AND EXPECTED SIGNIFICANCE - State the overarching research goals clearly and concisely - Explain the expected significance of the proposed work relative to the objectives in {{grant_info}} - Describe the perceived impact on the state of knowledge in the field - If this is a renewal proposal, describe how the work builds on and extends previous NASA-supported accomplishments - Ground all goals in the researcher's actual expertise and prior work from {{content}} 2. RELEVANCE TO NASA - Explicitly connect the proposed work to the specific objectives in {{grant_info}} - Reference the relevant NASA Mission Directorate and program priorities - Connect to present and/or future NASA programs and interests as described in the NASA Strategic Plan or soliciting directorate documents referenced in {{grant_info}} - Explain why NASA — rather than another funding agency — is the appropriate sponsor for this work - Use the exact program terminology and priority areas from {{grant_info}} 3. TECHNICAL APPROACH AND METHODOLOGY a. PROPOSED EXPERIMENTAL DESIGNS, METHODS, AND TECHNIQUES - Describe the specific methods, techniques, and approaches for achieving the proposed goals - Ground all methodology in the researcher's actual capabilities from {{content}} - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} — do NOT reframe as AI/ML unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML as core methodology - For each major task or objective, describe: what will be done, how it will be done, and why this approach is appropriate b. HARDWARE OR SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT - Describe any hardware or software development, construction, or fabrication required - Specify design requirements, performance targets, and development approach - If no hardware/software development is required, state this explicitly c. SOURCES OF ERROR AND UNCERTAINTY - Identify the primary sources of error and uncertainty in the proposed approach - Explain what effect they may have on the robustness of potential results or conclusions - Describe how errors will be quantified, bounded, or mitigated d. RESILIENCE OF THE APPROACH - Describe complementary measurements, confirming tests, or alternative approaches - Identify likely pitfalls of the proposed approach and how they will be addressed - Explain what will be done if a primary approach does not yield expected results e. SPECIAL CAPABILITIES AND FACILITIES - Identify any special capabilities, facilities, or equipment that provide advantages for this work - Reference specific capabilities from {{content}} that make this team uniquely qualified 4. TASK FLOW AND INTEGRATION - Describe how the different tasks flow into one another - Identify dependencies between tasks and how they are managed - Present as a logical sequence that demonstrates a coherent research plan - Include key milestones and decision points 5. PRIOR WORK AND PRELIMINARY RESULTS - Summarize relevant prior work and preliminary results from {{content}} - For each result, connect it to a specific proposed task or objective - Include quantitative performance metrics where available in {{content}} - Demonstrate feasibility of the proposed approach based on prior accomplishments - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data or citations not in {{content}} 6. TEAM QUALIFICATIONS AND MANAGEMENT - Briefly describe the qualifications of the PI and key team members as relevant to the proposed work - Explain the management structure and how the team will coordinate - Identify any collaborators and their specific roles and contributions - Note any partnerships with NASA centers, if applicable WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert peer reviewers who are specialists in the relevant NASA program area - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data, performance metrics, or citations not in {{content}} - If {{content}} does not provide sufficient detail for a subsection, note what additional information would strengthen it - Proposals shall not include references to materials outside the proposal for information needed to understand it — peer reviewers have no obligation to read external materials - Do NOT include URLs to external websites in the body of the proposal - Define all acronyms on first use - Propose fresh, new ideas — NASA reviewers look for innovation beyond slight modifications of prior work NASA EVALUATION CRITERIA TO ADDRESS (per NASA Guidebook Section 4.2): - Scientific, technical, and/or educational merit of the proposed investigation - Relevance of the proposed work to NASA's programs and/or interests - Reasonableness of the proposed budget relative to the proposed work - Qualifications of the proposing team OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Scientific/Technical/Management Plan" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for sub-subsections - Use

for paragraph content - Use

    /
      and
    1. for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 12-15 pages (7,000-10,000 words) — the NASA standard page limit for this section is 15 pages per the Guidebook, though the NOFO may specify otherwise
